input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-295
cat >> spec/foundation/11-dom-utilities.md <<'MD'

## 13. Signature Rasterization

`WebSignatureRasterizer` is the browser implementation of the
`ars_core::SignatureRasterizer` capability (see `01-architecture.md` §2.2.9). It
turns vector strokes into encoded PNG/JPEG bytes using an **offscreen
`<canvas>`** — no on-screen element required.

### 13.1 Behavior

Given `strokes: &[Vec<RasterPoint>]` and a `RasterSpec`, `rasterize`:

1. Creates a detached `<canvas>` via `document.createElement("canvas")` sized to
   `spec.width` × `spec.height`, and acquires its `2d` context.
2. Fills `spec.background` (a CSS color) when set; otherwise leaves the surface
   transparent (PNG) — JPEG has no alpha, so an unset background renders as the
   platform default.
3. Strokes each polyline **segment by segment**, setting `lineWidth` per segment
   to `spec.pen_width × pressure_scale`, where `pressure_scale` maps the average
   endpoint pressure to `0.5..=1.0` so a segment is always visible and firmer
   presses render thicker. `lineCap`/`lineJoin` are `round`.
4. Encodes via `canvas.toDataURL` (PNG, or JPEG honoring `spec.quality`), then
   decodes the base64 data URL to raw bytes with `window.atob`.

Returns `RasterImage { format, bytes }`, or `RasterError::Backend(_)` if any
browser step fails. On non-wasm targets the implementation returns
`RasterError::Unsupported`.

### 13.2 Reusers

| Helper                  | Spec callers                                                            |
| ----------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `WebSignatureRasterizer` | `SignaturePad` raster export (`components/specialized/signature-pad.md`) |
MD

content.text:
The `SignatureRasterizer` capability is fully implemented across all three crates and verified end-to-end (including real-browser wasm tests). Here is the summary for your review.

## SignatureRasterizer — injected raster export (full stack in #295)

**Design:** mirrors the existing `PlatformEffects` pattern — a neutral capability trait in `ars-core`, implemented by `ars-dom` for the browser, injected explicitly. Pressure-aware per your choice.

### `ars-core` (new [raster.rs](crates/ars-core/src/raster.rs))
- `SignatureRasterizer` trait + `NullSignatureRasterizer` (SSR/test no-op → `Unsupported`).
- Neutral types so `ars-dom` needn't depend on `ars-components`: `RasterPoint { x, y, pressure }`, `RasterSpec` (+builder), `RasterFormat {Png,Jpeg}`, `RasterImage`, `RasterError` (`impl core::error::Error`). Re-exported from `lib.rs`. **100% coverage.**

### `ars-components` ([signature_pad/mod.rs](crates/ars-components/src/specialized/signature_pad/mod.rs))
- `SignatureData::export_raster(&dyn SignatureRasterizer, &RasterSpec)` forwarding pressure-weighted points; `Api::export`/`Api::export_raster` delegators. Raster is impossible to call without a backend — MISU preserved, still no panic. `export` stays total (`Svg`/`Points`). **99.73% coverage** (only the pre-existing unreachable `DrawMove` guard).

### `ars-dom` (new [signature_raster.rs](crates/ars-dom/src/signature_raster.rs))
- `WebSignatureRasterizer`: offscreen `<canvas>` → per-segment pressure-scaled width (0.5–1.0× pen width, never zero) → `toDataURL` → `atob` decode to bytes. Non-wasm returns `Unsupported`.
- Added `CanvasRenderingContext2d` + `HtmlCanvasElement` to web-sys features (verified API against docs.rs for 0.3.99 — `set_stroke_style_str`, `to_data_url_with_type_and_encoder_options`, `Window::atob`). No new crates.
- **3 wasm browser tests pass** (`wasm-pack test --headless --chrome`): PNG magic, JPEG magic, blank-image.

### Specs synced
[01-architecture.md](spec/foundation/01-architecture.md) §2.2.9 (trait + types), [11-dom-utilities.md](spec/foundation/11-dom-utilities.md) §13 (WebSignatureRasterizer), core + both adapter [signature-pad.md](spec/components/specialized/signature-pad.md) export sections (replaced the earlier superset-enum framing).

### Verification
- `ars-core`/`ars-components`/`ars-dom` tests pass; **3 browser wasm tests pass**.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` clean (native + wasm32).
- `cargo doc` clean (missing_docs gate); `spec validate` + `compile-snippets` pass.

Reserved for post-approval: full `cargo xci-fast` (incl. native coverage gate + error-variant-coverage), commit, PR.

This is a larger change now spanning `ars-core` + `ars-components` + `ars-dom`. **No commit/push yet** — want me to proceed to `cargo xci-fast`, then commit and open the PR (`Closes #295`, `snapshot-reviewed` label) and start the Codex review loop? Note the wasm coverage path for `ars-dom` is measured by CI's nightly wasm pipeline, not `xci-fast`.

content.text:
Good design question. My recommendation: **keep `Backend(String)`**. Here's the reasoning.

## The constraints that decide it

1. **`ars-core` is `no_std` + alloc.** `Box<dyn core::error::Error + Send + Sync + 'static>` does work here (`core::error::Error` is stable, `Box` is in `alloc`), so that's not the blocker.

2. **The blocker is what the only backend actually produces.** Every failure path in `WebSignatureRasterizer` originates from a `web_sys` `JsValue` (`create_element`, `get_context`, `toDataURL`, `atob` all return `Result<_, JsValue>`). **`JsValue` does not implement `std::error::Error`** — it's an arbitrary JS value. So there is no underlying `Error` to preserve: I'd have to stringify the `JsValue` and wrap it in a synthetic error type just to box it. `Box<dyn Error>` would carry a boxed-string-error, not a real source — buying nothing over `String` while adding indirection.

3. **`Box<dyn Error>` forces dropping `Clone + PartialEq + Eq`** from `RasterError` (trait objects are none of those). Today the tests assert `Err(RasterError::Unsupported)` by equality, and `Eq`/`Clone` are cheap, useful guarantees for consumers. Switching means `matches!` everywhere and no cloning — a real ergonomic regression for no payoff.

4. **Consistency:** `ars-core`'s existing `ComponentError` is a plain enum (`impl core::error::Error`), not a `Box<dyn Error>` wrapper — `String`-style payloads match the crate's no_std error convention.

## When `Box<dyn Error>` *would* win

If a backend produced a real typed `std::error::Error` you wanted to preserve for `source()` chaining / `downcast` — e.g. a future **native** rasterizer using the `image` crate (`ImageError: Error`). That's plausible later, but:
- it's not implemented (YAGNI), and
- it'd still leave the web backend synthesizing a string.

If/when that native backend lands, the cleaner move is a dedicated typed variant (or a feature-gated `source`), decided with a concrete error type in hand — not a speculative `Box<dyn Error>` now that costs `Eq`/`Clone` for every caller.

## Verdict

`Backend(String)` is the right fit: lossless for the only backend (JsValue → message), keeps `RasterError: Clone + PartialEq + Eq`, stays `no_std`, matches `ComponentError`. I'd keep it as-is.

Want me to leave it, or do you want the `Box<dyn Error>` form regardless (accepting the loss of `Eq`/`Clone`)?

lastPrompt: It would make sense to have a `Backend(Box<dyn Error>)` instead of `Backend(String)` for the RasterError?

lastPrompt: Do we have other formats available in the browser like webp?

content.text:
Browser `canvas.toDataURL(type)` only reliably encodes three raster types, and WebP has a real cross-browser trap:

| Format | Chrome/Edge/Opera | Firefox | Safari |
|---|---|---|---|
| `image/png` | ✅ (default) | ✅ | ✅ |
| `image/jpeg` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `image/webp` | ✅ | ✅ (v96+) | ❌ |
| `image/avif` | ❌ (no canvas encode) | ❌ | ❌ |

So PNG/JPEG (what we have) are universal; **WebP works everywhere except Safari**. AVIF isn't a canvas-encode option anywhere.

**The trap:** per the HTML spec, if a browser doesn't support the requested type, `toDataURL` *silently falls back to `image/png`* — no error. So `export_raster(Webp)` on Safari would return PNG bytes while our `RasterImage.format` still claimed `Webp` — a lie. (This isn't a problem today: PNG/JPEG are universal, so my current `format: spec.format` is always accurate.)

If we add WebP, the honest fix is to **derive `RasterImage.format` from the returned data URL's actual MIME** (`data:image/<fmt>;base64,…`) instead of assuming `spec.format`. Then WebP-capable browsers get WebP, Safari transparently gets PNG, and `RasterImage.format` always tells the truth. WebP also honors `spec.quality` like JPEG. Cost: one enum variant + a tiny MIME-parse in the web impl + a Safari caveat in the doc/spec (and the wasm test asserts "webp *or* png" since CI's browser may vary).

4. Encodes via `canvas.toDataURL` (PNG, or JPEG honoring `spec.quality`), then
   decodes the base64 data URL to raw bytes with `window.atob`.

Returns `RasterImage { format, bytes }`, or `RasterError::Backend(_)` if any

input.new_string:
4. Encodes via `canvas.toDataURL` using `spec.format.mime()` (PNG, or JPEG/WebP
   honoring `spec.quality`), then decodes the base64 data URL to raw bytes with
   `window.atob`.

Returns `RasterImage { format, bytes }`, or `RasterError::Backend(_)` if any
browser step fails. On non-wasm targets the implementation returns
`RasterError::Unsupported`.

**Honest format reporting.** A browser that cannot encode the requested type
silently substitutes PNG (notably Safari for WebP). The implementation therefore
derives `RasterImage::format` from the returned data URL's own MIME via
`RasterFormat::from_mime`, not from the request — so a WebP request that fell
back to PNG reports `Png` with PNG bytes, never a mislabeled `Webp`.

file.content:
`spec.width` × `spec.height`, and acquires its `2d` context.
2. Fills `spec.background` (a CSS color) when set; otherwise leaves the surface
   transparent (PNG) — JPEG has no alpha, so an unset background renders as the
   platform default.
3. Strokes each polyline **segment by segment**, setting `lineWidth` per segment
   to `spec.pen_width × pressure_scale`, where `pressure_scale` maps the average
   endpoint pressure to `0.5..=1.0` so a segment is always visible and firmer
   presses render thicker. `lineCap`/`lineJoin` are `round`.
4. Encodes via `canvas.toDataURL` (PNG, or JPEG honoring `spec.quality`), then
   decodes the base64 data URL to raw bytes with `window.atob`.

Returns `RasterImage { format, bytes }`, or `RasterError::Backend(_)` if any
browser step fails. On non-wasm targets the implementation returns
`RasterError::Unsupported`.

### 13.2 Reusers

| Helper                   | Spec callers                                                             |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `WebSignatureRasterizer` | `SignaturePad` raster export (`components/specialized/signature-pad.md`) |

input.old_string:
4. Encodes via `canvas.toDataURL` (PNG, or JPEG honoring `spec.quality`), then
   decodes the base64 data URL to raw bytes with `window.atob`.

Returns `RasterImage { format, bytes }`, or `RasterError::Backend(_)` if any
browser step fails. On non-wasm targets the implementation returns
`RasterError::Unsupported`.
input.new_string:
4. Encodes via `canvas.toDataURL` using `spec.format.mime()` (PNG, or JPEG/WebP
   honoring `spec.quality`), then decodes the base64 data URL to raw bytes with
   `window.atob`.

Returns `RasterImage { format, bytes }`, or `RasterError::Backend(_)` if any
browser step fails. On non-wasm targets the implementation returns
`RasterError::Unsupported`.

**Honest format reporting.** A browser that cannot encode the requested type
silently substitutes PNG (notably Safari for WebP). The implementation derives
`RasterImage::format` from the returned data URL's own MIME via
`RasterFormat::from_mime`, not from the request — so a WebP request that fell
back to PNG reports `Png` with PNG bytes, never a mislabeled `Webp`.

content.text:
WebP support is complete and fully verified. Summary of this addition:

## WebP + honest format detection

**`ars-core`** ([raster.rs](crates/ars-core/src/raster.rs)): added `RasterFormat::Webp` plus `RasterFormat::mime()` / `from_mime()` helpers (the round-trip used for honest detection). 8 tests; ars-core aggregate coverage **96.29%** (threshold 75%).

**`ars-dom`** ([signature_raster.rs](crates/ars-dom/src/signature_raster.rs)): WebP routed through the quality encoder; **`RasterImage::format` is now derived from the returned data URL's actual MIME** (`from_mime`), so Safari's silent PNG fallback is reported truthfully as `Png`, never mislabeled `Webp`. Added a 4th wasm browser test asserting format↔magic-byte consistency — **all 4 pass in headless Chrome** (real WebP `RIFF…WEBP` verified).

**Specs**: `RasterFormat` enum (01-architecture §2.2.9), signature-pad export note, 11-dom-utilities §13 (honest-reporting paragraph), both adapter specs — all now say PNG/JPEG/WebP with the Safari caveat.

**Verification**: clippy `-D warnings` clean (native all-features + wasm32); `spec validate` + `compile-snippets` pass; all unit/wasm tests green.

The full feature set is now: vector export (`Svg`/`Points`, total) in the agnostic core, plus injected raster export (`Png`/`Jpeg`/`Webp`, pressure-aware, honest format) via `SignatureRasterizer` with `ars-dom`'s `WebSignatureRasterizer`.

**No commit/push yet.** Ready for the final steps pending your go-ahead:
1. `cargo xci-fast` (full pre-push gate incl. native coverage + error-variant-coverage), fix anything it flags.
2. Commit + PR to `main` (`Closes #295`, `snapshot-reviewed` label).
3. `waiting-for-codex-review` loop until 👍.

Shall I proceed with all three?

lastPrompt: Do we have other formats available in the browser like webp?

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

## SKILLS TO INVOKE

Always invoke with Skill() tool:
- Skill(rust-router) - First, to get routing
- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

attachment.snippet:
1	//! Platform-agnostic interface for rasterizing vector strokes into an encoded
2	//! image (PNG/JPEG).
3	//!
4	//! The agnostic core models a signature (and similar freehand input) as vector
5	//! strokes. Turning those into a raster image requires a pixel surface and an
6	//! encoder, which only the platform provides — so, like
7	//! [`PlatformEffects`](crate::PlatformEffects), rasterization goes through a
8	//! trait resolved from the adapter layer:
9	//!
10	//! - `ars-dom` provides `WebSignatureRasterizer` (browser `<canvas>`).
11	//! - [`NullSignatureRasterizer`] is the no-op for SSR and unit tests.
12	//!
13	//! The contract is deliberately neutral — it takes pressure-weighted points and
14	//! a [`RasterSpec`], never any component-specific type — so the lower-level
15	//! `ars-dom` crate can implement it without depending on the component crates.
16	
17	use alloc::{string::String, vec::Vec};
18	use core::fmt;
19	
20	/// A sampled stroke point for rasterization: position plus normalized pressure.
21	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq)]
22	pub struct RasterPoint {
23	    /// The x coordinate of the point, in canvas pixels.
24	    pub x: f64,
25	
26	    /// The y coordinate of the point, in canvas pixels.
27	    pub y: f64,
28	
29	    /// Normalized pressure in `0.0..=1.0`; scales the rendered stroke width so
30	    /// firmer presses draw thicker segments.
31	    pub pressure: f64,
32	}
33	
34	/// Encoded image format produced by a [`SignatureRasterizer`].
35	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
36	pub enum RasterFormat {
37	    /// PNG (lossless, supports transparency).
38	    Png,
39	
40	    /// JPEG (lossy; honors [`RasterSpec::quality`], has no alpha channel).
41	    Jpeg,
42	
43	    /// WebP (honors [`RasterSpec::quality`]). Not encodable by every browser
44	    /// canvas — Safari silently falls back to PNG — so a rasterizer requesting
45	    /// WebP MUST report the format it actually produced (see
46	    /// [`RasterImage::format`]), not the requested one.
47	    Webp,
48	}
49	
50	impl RasterFormat {
51	    /// The MIME type string used to request this format from a canvas encoder.
52	    #[must_use]
53	    pub const fn mime(self) -> &'static str {
54	        match self {
55	            Self::Png => "image/png",
56	            Self::Jpeg => "image/jpeg",
57	            Self::Webp => "image/webp",
58	        }
59	    }
60	
61	    /// Parses a MIME type — e.g. the one embedded in a `data:` URL returned by a
62	    /// canvas — back into a [`RasterFormat`]. Returns `None` for unrecognized
63	    /// types, letting callers fall back to the requested format.
64	    #[must_use]
65	    pub fn from_mime(mime: &str) -> Option<Self> {
66	        match mime {
67	            "image/png" => Some(Self::Png),
68	            "image/jpeg" => Some(Self::Jpeg),
69	            "image/webp" => Some(Self::Webp),
70	            _ => None,
71	        }
72	    }
73	}
74	
75	/// Rendering parameters for a rasterization request.
76	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
77	pub struct RasterSpec {
78	    /// Output bitmap width in pixels.
79	    pub width: u32,
80	
81	    /// Output bitmap height in pixels.
82	    pub height: u32,
83	
84	    /// Stroke color as a CSS color string.
85	    pub pen_color: String,
86	
87	    /// Base stroke width in pixels (scaled per point by [`RasterPoint::pressure`]).
88	    pub pen_width: f64,
89	
90	    /// Background fill as a CSS color string. `None` leaves the surface
91	    /// transparent (PNG); JPEG has no alpha, so a `None` background renders
92	    /// as the platform default (typically black).
93	    pub background: Option<String>,
94	
95	    /// Encoded output format.
96	    pub format: RasterFormat,
97	
98	    /// Encoder quality in `0.0..=1.0` for [`RasterFormat::Jpeg`]; ignored for PNG.
99	    pub quality: f64,
100	}
101	
102	impl RasterSpec {
103	    /// Creates a [`RasterSpec`] for a `width` × `height` PNG with the default
104	    /// black pen, 2px width, transparent background, and 0.92 JPEG quality.
105	    #[must_use]
106	    pub fn new(width: u32, height: u32) -> Self {
107	        Self {
108	            width,
109	            height,
110	            pen_color: String::from("#000000"),
111	            pen_width: 2.0,
112	            background: None,
113	            format: RasterFormat::Png,
114	            quality: 0.92,
115	        }
116	    }
117	
118	    /// Sets [`pen_color`](Self::pen_color).
119	    #[must_use]
120	    pub fn pen_color(mut self, pen_color: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
121	        self.pen_color = pen_color.into();
122	        self
123	    }
124	
125	    /// Sets [`pen_width`](Self::pen_width).
126	    #[must_use]
127	    pub const fn pen_width(mut self, pen_width: f64) -> Self {
128	        self.pen_width = pen_width;
129	        self
130	    }
131	
132	    /// Sets [`background`](Self::background) to an opaque CSS color.
133	    #[must_use]
134	    pub fn background(mut self, background: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
135	        self.background = Some(background.into());
136	        self
137	    }
138	
139	    /// Sets [`format`](Self::format).
140	    #[must_use]
141	    pub const fn format(mut self, format: RasterFormat) -> Self {
142	        self.format = format;
143	        self
144	    }
145	
146	    /// Sets [`quality`](Self::quality) (JPEG only).
147	    #[must_use]
148	    pub const fn quality(mut self, quality: f64) -> Self {
149	        self.quality = quality;
150	        self
151	    }
152	}
153	
154	/// An encoded raster image produced by a [`SignatureRasterizer`].
155	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
156	pub struct RasterImage {
157	    /// The format the bytes are encoded in.
158	    pub format: RasterFormat,
159	    /// The encoded image bytes (e.g. PNG/JPEG file contents).

input.description: Inspect ColorValue API and SignaturePad pen_color modeling

lastPrompt: Should this new raster code use our existing color facilities instead of using plaing String? Actual question, don't change any code yet

content.text:
Good instinct to check for reuse, but here `String` (CSS color) is the right fit — `ColorValue` would actually hurt. Reasoning:

**Source and sink are both CSS strings.**
- The `SignaturePad` component already models pen color as `pub pen_color: String` ("CSS color string", default `"#000000"`) in both `Props` and `Context` (spec §1.3/§1.4). So the value arrives as a CSS string.
- The canvas sink — `ctx.set_stroke_style_str(&str)` / `set_fill_style_str(&str)` — also takes a CSS string.

So `RasterSpec.pen_color: String` is the same representation at both ends. Inserting `ColorValue` in the middle means: component `String` → `ColorValue` → back to `String` for the canvas — two conversions around a value that's a string the whole way.

**`ColorValue` can't represent what a CSS pen color can.** `ColorValue` is HSL+alpha, and its only string parser is `from_hex` (returns `Option`, hex-only). It can't carry named colors (`"red"`), `rgb()/hsl()` inputs, `currentColor`, or theme tokens — all valid, useful pen colors an author might set. Routing through `ColorValue` would silently drop those (`from_hex` → `None`).

**`ColorValue` earns its place where color is *manipulated*, not passed through.** ColorPicker / ColorArea / ColorSlider use `ColorValue` because they need HSL channels, 2D area mapping, format conversions, channel math. SignaturePad doesn't manipulate the pen color — it's an author-set token forwarded to a CSS API. Different role, different right type. (Layering isn't the blocker — `RasterSpec` is in `ars-core` and could use `ColorValue`; it's the semantic mismatch.)

**Verdict:** keep `pen_color: String` / `background: Option<String>`. It matches how the component already specs pen color, matches the canvas API, and preserves full CSS-color expressiveness.

The only scenario where `ColorValue` would fit is if you wanted pen color **modeled as `ColorValue` end-to-end** — i.e., change the SignaturePad component's `pen_color` field to `ColorValue` too (e.g., to drive it from a ColorPicker), converting to a CSS string only at the canvas boundary via `to_hex(true)`. That's coherent but a spec change to the component, and it trades away named-color/`currentColor` support. Want me to keep `String` (my recommendation), or pursue that end-to-end `ColorValue` redesign?

content.text:
Short answer: a `CssColor` that **exhaustively** types every CSS color is the wrong goal — it's effectively a CSS color engine — but a small typed wrapper *could* make sense later as its own foundation effort. Not in this PR.

## Why exhaustive typing doesn't work

CSS color (Color Module L4/L5) is huge and, worse, partly **unresolvable outside the browser**:
- Families: named (~150) + `transparent`, hex (3/4/6/8), `rgb()/hsl()/hwb()`, `lab()/lch()/oklab()/oklch()`, `color(display-p3 …)`, `color-mix()`, relative color (`rgb(from … )`), system colors (`Canvas`, `ButtonText`).
- Context-dependent tokens: `currentColor`, `var(--token)`, `light-dark()`, system colors — these **only resolve at paint time** against the computed style + custom-property registry. A standalone Rust value can't evaluate them; it can only carry them opaquely.
- It's a **moving target** — `oklch`, relative color, `color-mix` are recent; a closed enum would perpetually lag CSS.

So "all of CSS color, typed" means either reimplementing/maintaining a CSS color parser+resolver (out of scope for a component library) or having an `Other(String)` escape hatch that immediately swallows the long tail — at which point it's "typed where convenient, string otherwise," not exhaustive.

## We already have the right two-tier model

- **`ColorValue` (HSL+alpha)** — for colors we *compute and manipulate* (ColorPicker area/sliders/channels, conversions, contrast). This is where typing earns its keep, and it's already used there.
- **CSS-color `String`** — for colors we *pass straight to the DOM* (pen color, backgrounds). The browser is the authority; a string supports 100% of CSS color **including `var(--brand)`, `currentColor`, theming, and future syntax** that no closed type could.

These cover the two real roles. The split isn't an inconsistency — it's "value vs. pass-through token."

## If you ever do want a typed boundary

The defensible shape is a **small, non-exhaustive** enum, introduced when a concrete consumer needs it (e.g., validating authored colors, or converting at many call sites):

```rust
enum CssColor {
    Value(ColorValue),  // hex/rgb/hsl families — typed & manipulable
    Keyword(NamedColor),// named + currentColor + transparent
    Raw(String),        // escape hatch: var(), oklch(), color-mix(), future syntax
}
```

That's a **cross-cutting vocabulary type** touching `ColorValue`, every color component, and theming — a foundation-level decision deserving its own spec task and review, **not** a rider on the SignaturePad rasterizer PR. Bolting it on here would be scope creep and would churn unrelated APIs.

## Recommendation for #295

Keep `pen_color: String` / `background: Option<String>`. If the typed-`CssColor` idea has legs, I'd capture it as a separate foundation issue (with the `Value | Keyword | Raw` sketch and the "don't try to be exhaustive" rationale) so it's designed deliberately.

Want me to (a) keep `String` and move on to finishing #295, or (b) also write up that future `CssColor` design as a spec note / issue before we wrap?

attachment.snippet:
1	//! `SignaturePad` component state machine and connect API.
2	//!
3	//! The `SignaturePad` captures freehand pointer/touch input as vector path data.
4	//! It records strokes as point arrays (with pressure and timestamp), supports
5	//! undo and clear, and exposes the accumulated [`SignatureData`] for form
6	//! submission (as an SVG path) and export.
7	//!
8	//! The agnostic core owns the stroke data model, the drawing state machine,
9	//! `min_distance` point culling, stroke validation, and the ARIA/`data-ars-*`
10	//! attribute surface. It does **not** touch the live `<canvas>`: pointer
11	//! capture, device-pixel-ratio scaling, bounding-rect measurement, and raster
12	//! rasterization (PNG/JPEG) belong to the framework adapters.
13	//!
14	//! Like the other component machines, the core only emits typed [`Effect`]
15	//! intents; the adapter fulfils them. [`Effect::DrawingListeners`] tells the
16	//! adapter to attach global pointer-drag listeners (e.g. via
17	//! `PlatformEffects::track_pointer_drag`) that dispatch [`Event::DrawMove`] and
18	//! [`Event::DrawEnd`]; [`Effect::AnnounceProvided`] and
19	//! [`Effect::AnnounceCleared`] tell the adapter to announce the corresponding
20	//! message into a polite `aria-live` region.
21	
22	use alloc::{string::String, vec, vec::Vec};
23	use core::fmt::{self, Debug, Write as _};
24	
25	use ars_core::{
26	    AriaAttr, AttrMap, Bindable, ComponentIds, ComponentMessages, ComponentPart, ConnectApi, Env,
27	    HasId, HtmlAttr, Locale, MessageFn, PendingEffect, RasterError, RasterImage, RasterPoint,
28	    RasterSpec, SignatureRasterizer, TransitionPlan,
29	};
30	
31	/// A single point in a signature stroke.
32	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq)]
33	pub struct SignaturePoint {
34	    /// The x coordinate of the point.
35	    pub x: f64,
36	
37	    /// The y coordinate of the point.
38	    pub y: f64,
39	
40	    /// Pressure from 0.0 to 1.0 (for pressure-sensitive input).
41	    pub pressure: f64,
42	
43	    /// Timestamp in milliseconds.
44	    pub timestamp: f64,
45	}
46	
47	/// A continuous stroke (pen-down to pen-up).
48	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
49	pub struct SignatureStroke {
50	    /// The points in the stroke.
51	    pub points: Vec<SignaturePoint>,
52	}
53	
54	/// The complete signature data.
55	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Default)]
56	pub struct SignatureData {
57	    /// The strokes in the signature.
58	    pub strokes: Vec<SignatureStroke>,
59	}
60	
61	impl SignatureData {
62	    /// Check if the signature data is empty.
63	    #[must_use]
64	    pub const fn is_empty(&self) -> bool {
65	        self.strokes.is_empty()
66	    }
67	
68	    /// Convert to SVG path data string.
69	    ///
70	    /// Each stroke contributes a `moveto` to its first point followed by a
71	    /// `lineto` to each subsequent point. Strokes are concatenated directly,
72	    /// so the resulting path describes the whole signature as one `d` value.
73	    #[must_use]
74	    pub fn to_svg_path(&self) -> String {
75	        let mut path = String::new();
76	
77	        for stroke in &self.strokes {
78	            let Some(first) = stroke.points.first() else {
79	                continue;
80	            };
81	
82	            let _ = write!(path, "M{:.1},{:.1}", first.x, first.y);
83	
84	            for point in &stroke.points[1..] {
85	                let _ = write!(path, " L{:.1},{:.1}", point.x, point.y);
86	            }
87	        }
88	        path
89	    }
90	
91	    /// Total number of points across all strokes.
92	    #[must_use]
93	    pub fn point_count(&self) -> usize {
94	        self.strokes.iter().map(|stroke| stroke.points.len()).sum()
95	    }
96	
97	    /// Export the signature in one of the resolution-independent formats the
98	    /// agnostic core can produce.
99	    ///
100	    /// For [`SignatureFormat::Svg`] the core generates the markup from stroke
101	    /// data; for [`SignatureFormat::Points`] the raw [`SignaturePoint`] vectors
102	    /// are returned directly, enabling server-side vector reconstruction
103	    /// regardless of display resolution.
104	    ///
105	    /// Raster formats (PNG/JPEG/base64-PNG) require an off-screen canvas and are
106	    /// therefore *not* part of [`SignatureFormat`]: framework adapters expose
107	    /// their own superset format enum for those, rendering strokes onto a canvas
108	    /// and delegating the vector formats here.
109	    #[must_use]
110	    pub fn export(&self, format: SignatureFormat) -> SignatureExport {
111	        match format {
112	            SignatureFormat::Svg => SignatureExport::Svg(self.to_svg_path()),
113	
114	            SignatureFormat::Points => SignatureExport::Points(
115	                self.strokes
116	                    .iter()
117	                    .map(|stroke| stroke.points.clone())
118	                    .collect(),
119	            ),
120	        }
121	    }
122	
123	    /// Rasterize the signature into an encoded image (PNG/JPEG) via an injected
124	    /// [`SignatureRasterizer`].
125	    ///
126	    /// Raster output needs a pixel surface, which the agnostic core does not
127	    /// have — so the caller supplies the platform rasterizer (`ars-dom`'s
128	    /// `WebSignatureRasterizer` in the browser). The per-point
129	    /// [`pressure`](SignaturePoint::pressure) is forwarded to the rasterizer so
130	    /// firmer presses render as thicker strokes. Requiring the rasterizer as an
131	    /// argument keeps raster export impossible to call without a backend
132	    /// (make-invalid-states-unrepresentable) instead of panicking.
133	    ///
134	    /// # Errors
135	    ///
136	    /// Propagates the rasterizer's [`RasterError`] — notably
137	    /// [`RasterError::Unsupported`] under SSR/tests.
138	    pub fn export_raster(
139	        &self,
140	        rasterizer: &dyn SignatureRasterizer,
141	        spec: &RasterSpec,
142	    ) -> Result<RasterImage, RasterError> {
143	        let strokes = self
144	            .strokes
145	            .iter()
146	            .map(|stroke| {
147	                stroke
148	                    .points
149	                    .iter()
150	                    .map(|&SignaturePoint { x, y, pressure, .. }| RasterPoint { x, y, pressure })
151	                    .collect()
152	            })
153	            .collect::<Vec<_>>();
154	
155	        rasterizer.rasterize(&strokes, spec)
156	    }
157	}
158	
159	/// Resolution-independent export formats the agnostic core can produce.
160	///
161	/// Raster formats (PNG/JPEG/base64-PNG) need a live canvas and live in the
162	/// adapter layer's own export format enum, not here — keeping this type free of
163	/// variants the core cannot fulfil (make-invalid-states-unrepresentable, so
164	/// [`SignatureData::export`] is total with no unreachable arm).
165	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
166	pub enum SignatureFormat {
167	    /// SVG path markup.
168	    Svg,
169	
170	    /// Raw point data.
171	    Points,
172	}
173	
174	/// Exported signature data in one of the [`SignatureFormat`] variants.
175	///
176	/// Mirrors [`SignatureFormat`]: every variant here is something the core can
177	/// actually return. Adapters define their own export type for raster output.
178	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
179	pub enum SignatureExport {
180	    /// SVG markup string.
181	    Svg(String),
182	
183	    /// Raw point data for vector reconstruction.
184	    /// Each inner `Vec` is one continuous stroke.
185	    Points(Vec<Vec<SignaturePoint>>),
186	}
187	
188	/// The states for the `SignaturePad` component.
189	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
190	pub enum State {
191	    /// No strokes, canvas is blank.
192	    Idle,
193	
194	    /// The user is actively drawing (pointer/touch down).
195	    Drawing,
196	
197	    /// At least one stroke has been completed.
198	    Completed,
199	}
200	
201	/// The events for the `SignaturePad` component.
202	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq)]
203	pub enum Event {
204	    /// Pointer/touch down on the canvas.
205	    DrawStart {
206	        /// The x coordinate of the draw start.
207	        x: f64,
208	
209	        /// The y coordinate of the draw start.

... [1855 lines truncated] ...

=== RUST SKILLS DISPLAY FORMAT ===
When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

## SKILLS TO INVOKE

Always invoke with Skill() tool:
- Skill(rust-router) - First, to get routing
- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

===================================

cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-295
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Implement SignaturePad agnostic core (#295)

Add the framework-agnostic `SignaturePad` state machine in `ars-components`
(`specialized/signature_pad`): Idle/Drawing/Completed states, pressure-aware
stroke capture with `min_distance` culling, undo/clear, controlled/uncontrolled
data binding with prop-sync, SVG-path form value, and the full ARIA/`data-ars-*`
connect surface. Effects are typed markers (`DrawingListeners`,
`AnnounceProvided`, `AnnounceCleared`) fulfilled by adapters.

State semantics are kept honest: undoing or tap-discarding the last stroke
returns to Idle (Completed always holds >=1 stroke), proven by unit + proptest
invariants.

Export:
- Vector formats (`Svg`, `Points`) are produced by the total
  `SignatureData::export` (no panicking arm).
- Raster (PNG/JPEG/WebP) is an injected platform capability: new
  `ars_core::SignatureRasterizer` trait + neutral `RasterPoint`/`RasterSpec`/
  `RasterImage`/`RasterFormat`/`RasterError` types (+ `NullSignatureRasterizer`),
  consumed via `SignatureData::export_raster`. `ars-dom` provides
  `WebSignatureRasterizer` (offscreen canvas, pressure-scaled stroke width,
  toDataURL+atob) reporting the format actually produced so a browser's silent
  WebP->PNG fallback is never mislabeled.

Tests: 64 unit/snapshot tests (17 committed `.snap` fixtures), spec-conformance
anatomy test, proptest invariants, ars-core raster unit tests, and 4 ars-dom
wasm browser tests. Specs synced: signature-pad core + adapter specs,
foundation 01-architecture (SignatureRasterizer capability) and
11-dom-utilities (WebSignatureRasterizer).

Closes #295

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

git log --oneline -1

## Summary

Implements the framework-agnostic `SignaturePad` core (GitHub issue #295, Epic #227) plus an injected raster-export capability spanning `ars-core` and `ars-dom`.

### `ars-components` — `specialized/signature_pad`
- `Idle`/`Drawing`/`Completed` state machine; pressure-aware stroke capture with `min_distance` culling; undo/clear; Focus/Blur.
- Controlled/uncontrolled `data` binding with `on_props_changed` prop-sync (`SyncData`/`SyncProps`), matching the sibling-component convention.
- Full ARIA/`data-ars-*` connect surface (7 parts: Root, Canvas, ClearTrigger, UndoTrigger, Label, Guide, HiddenInput); SVG-path hidden-input value.
- Typed marker effects (`DrawingListeners`, `AnnounceProvided`, `AnnounceCleared`) fulfilled by adapters.
- **State honesty:** undo/tap-discard of the last stroke returns to `Idle` (`Completed` always holds ≥1 stroke), enforced by unit + proptest invariants.

### Export
- Vector (`Svg`, `Points`) via the **total** `SignatureData::export` — no panicking/`unimplemented!` arm.
- Raster (PNG/JPEG/WebP) as an **injected platform capability** (MISU): new `ars_core::SignatureRasterizer` trait + neutral `RasterPoint`/`RasterSpec`/`RasterImage`/`RasterFormat`/`RasterError` (+ `NullSignatureRasterizer`), consumed via `SignatureData::export_raster`. `ars-dom`'s `WebSignatureRasterizer` renders an offscreen canvas (pressure-scaled stroke width) and reports the format **actually produced**, so a browser's silent WebP→PNG fallback (Safari) is never mislabeled.

### Tests
- 64 unit/snapshot tests (17 committed `.snap` fixtures), spec-conformance anatomy test, proptest invariants, `ars-core` raster unit tests, **4 `ars-dom` wasm browser tests** (PNG/JPEG/WebP magic bytes + honest-format fallback).

### Spec sync
- `components/specialized/signature-pad.md` (typed effects, prop-sync, state reconciliation, export); both adapter specs.
- `foundation/01-architecture.md` §2.2.9 (`SignatureRasterizer` capability) and `foundation/11-dom-utilities.md` §13 (`WebSignatureRasterizer`).

Closes #295

content.content: Launching skill: waiting-for-codex-review

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-295/.claude/skills/waiting-for-codex-review

# Waiting for Codex Review

Codex (the OpenAI Codex automated reviewer) reviews open PRs asynchronously. The reaction contract on the PR itself:

- **Run starts** → Codex adds 👀 (eyes). This is the "I'm looking" signal.
- **Run finishes — approved** → Codex *removes* 👀 and adds 👍 (thumbsup). No further action required.
- **Run finishes — changes requested** → Codex *removes* 👀 and posts one or more inline review threads. **No 👍 is added.** The disappearance of 👀 is the signal that this run finished with findings.
- **You push a fix** → Codex re-adds 👀 to start the next pass. The loop resumes.

The **👀-disappears** transition is load-bearing: it's how you distinguish "still reviewing" from "review done — go look at the threads". Don't keep waiting for 👍 once 👀 has dropped; switch to the thread-handling subroutine immediately.

This skill is the polling + response loop for that contract. You stay in it until 👍 lands.

## When to invoke

Invoke as soon as a PR is pushed and Codex is set up for the repository. Common trigger phrases:

- "wait for the codex review"
- "what does codex say about the PR"
- "address the codex feedback"
- "check the PR reviews"
- "is codex still reviewing"

If the user pushes a PR and gives no explicit next instruction, default to invoking this skill — the right next step is to watch for Codex.

## Reaction lookups: the exact data

PRs are issues for the reactions endpoint. The bot's login depends on the install — on installs that use the ChatGPT Codex connector it's `chatgpt-codex-connector`; on the standalone Codex Cloud install it may be `codex[bot]` or similar. The first poll of this loop **discovers the bot login** rather than hard-coding it: any review-comment author whose handle contains `codex` is treated as Codex.

```bash
# Reactions on the PR itself
gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<pr>/reactions
# → array of { user.login, content } where content ∈ {'+1','-1','laugh','confused','heart','hooray','rocket','eyes'}
# +1 == 👍, eyes == 👀

# Reviews and review threads
gh pr view <pr> --json reviews
gh api graphql -f query='
  query($owner:String!,$repo:String!,$n:Int!){
    repository(owner:$owner,name:$repo){
      pullRequest(number:$n){
        reviewThreads(first:100){
          nodes{
            id            # GraphQL node id — use this to RESOLVE
            isResolved
            comments(first:100){
              nodes{ databaseId author{login} path line body }
              # databaseId is the REST id — use this to REPLY
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }' -F owner=<owner> -F repo=<repo> -F n=<pr>
```

Keep two ids per thread straight: **`thread.id`** (GraphQL node id, opaque string starting with `PRRT_...`) is needed to *resolve* the thread; **`comment.databaseId`** (integer) is needed to *reply* in-line. They are not interchangeable.

## The loop

```dot
digraph codex_loop {
    poll [label="Poll reactions + threads", shape=diamond];
    thumbs [label="Codex 👍 on PR?", shape=diamond];
    eyes [label="Codex 👀 on PR?", shape=diamond];
    threads [label="Unresolved Codex threads?", shape=diamond];
    address [label="Address each thread (TDD)", shape=box];
    push [label="Commit + push fix", shape=box];
    reply [label="Reply inline + resolve thread", shape=box];
    trigger [label="Comment '@codex review'", shape=box];
    wait_review [label="Sleep — Codex still reviewing", shape=box];
    wait_start [label="Sleep — Codex hasn't started", shape=box];
    done [label="Done — hand back to user", shape=doublecircle];

    poll -> thumbs;
    thumbs -> done [label="yes"];
    thumbs -> eyes [label="no"];
    eyes -> wait_review [label="yes (still reviewing)"];
    eyes -> threads [label="no (👀 dropped → run finished)"];
    threads -> address [label="yes"];
    threads -> wait_start [label="no (pre-review, no run yet)"];
    wait_review -> poll;
    wait_start -> poll;
    address -> push -> reply -> trigger -> poll;
}
```

**Three terminal poll states matter:**

| State | What it means | What to do |
|---|---|---|
| 👍 present (👀 absent) | Codex approved | Done — hand back to user |
| 👀 present | Codex is actively reviewing right now | Sleep, re-poll |
| 👀 absent AND 👍 absent | Either Codex finished with findings, or it never started | Check threads — if any unresolved Codex thread exists, address them; otherwise treat as "not started yet" and sleep |

## Step-by-step

### 1. Identify the PR

If the user names the PR, use that number. Otherwise, fall back to the head branch:

```bash
gh pr view --json number,url,headRefName
```

Confirm the PR is open and was pushed in this session before entering the loop — don't enter the loop on a stale branch.

### 2. Kick off the initial review with `@codex review`

Codex does not always start a review automatically when a PR is opened. Some installs only trigger on explicit mention, others trigger automatically *most* of the time but not always. Either way, posting `@codex review` as a top-level comment right after the first push is the deterministic way to start the first review — and it's an idempotent no-op when Codex would have triggered on its own (Codex just sees the redundant mention and starts).

```bash
gh pr comment <pr> --body "@codex review"
```

Post this **once** immediately after entering the loop on a freshly-pushed PR, **before** the first snapshot/poll. This is the same trigger used in step 7 for subsequent passes — the difference is just timing (entry vs. after a fix push). If you entered the loop on a PR that already has a 👀 or 👍 reaction from Codex (e.g., the user invoked the skill mid-stream), skip this step — the review is already in flight.

### 3. Snapshot the current state

Before polling, capture the state so the next poll can detect *new* signals (not just any signals):

```text
seen_reactions := { (user, content) ... } from /issues/<pr>/reactions
seen_threads   := { thread.id ... } from the GraphQL query
```

### 4. Poll, decide, act

Read the **reaction set first**, threads second — the reaction is the run-state signal, the threads are the run *output*.

| 👀 on PR | 👍 on PR | Unresolved Codex threads | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| no | yes | (don't care) | Loop is done. Announce. |
| yes | no | (don't care) | Codex is reviewing right now. Sleep, then re-poll. |
| no | no | yes | Codex finished this run with findings — **the 👀 dropped, no 👍 was added**. Drop into the "address comments" subroutine. |
| no | no | no | Either Codex hasn't started yet (first poll after push), or every thread is already resolved and a fresh review is pending. Sleep, then re-poll. |

Decision-making notes:

- **The 👀 → ∅ transition is the "run finished with findings" signal.** Don't keep waiting for 👍 — switch to thread handling as soon as 👀 drops and threads exist.
- **Codex sometimes posts a top-level review body** ("LGTM" / a short summary) without 👍. Treat that as advisory — only the 👍 reaction is the terminal approval signal.
- **A new push does NOT reliably retrigger Codex.** The auto-retrigger fires sometimes but not always; do not rely on it. After every fix push + thread resolution, post `@codex review` as a fresh top-level comment to deterministically start the next pass (see step 7 of the response workflow). If 👀 does not reappear within ~3 minutes after the comment, double-check that the push actually landed on the PR branch (`gh pr view <pr> --json commits`).
- **Multiple Codex passes are normal.** The loop must tolerate Codex finding new issues introduced by your fix. Each pass is a fresh 👀 → (👍 | ∅ + threads) cycle, kicked off by a fresh `@codex review` comment.

### 5. Address review comments (the hard part)

Each unresolved Codex thread is a finding that must land a fix in *this* PR — no follow-up issues, no deferral. The bar is the same as the `post-implementation-audit` skill (read it if you haven't):

1. **Read the comment twice** before you start. Then read the surrounding 30 lines of the file. Codex's comments are usually right but sometimes context-thin — your job is to understand whether the suggested change is the *best outcome* or whether the underlying concern can be fixed differently. If you disagree, see "Disagreeing with Codex" below.
2. **Write the test first.** For every code change you make, add or update a named test that *fails on the current code* and *passes after the fix*. Skipping the test is what lets the regression come back silently on the next iteration. This applies even to "obvious" fixes — obvious code regresses too.
3. **Coverage must not regress.** Capture the current per-line / per-region / per-branch numbers on the touched module *before* changing anything. After the fix, re-run the same command. If any number drops, find the missing test and add it before committing.
4. **Workspace conventions still apply.** Lint flags, derive lists, `no_std` constraints, doc comments on public items, spec-as-contract — all unchanged. Codex's suggestion is a finding, not a license to skip the project rules.
5. **Commit with a thread-linked message.** The commit message names the issue Codex raised, not just the file you touched. Example: `fix(group): clamp role on legacy adapters (Codex review #648)`.

### 6. Push, reply inline, resolve

After pushing:

**Reply** on each thread you addressed. Use the first comment's `databaseId` as the parent:

```bash
gh api -X POST repos/<owner>/<repo>/pulls/<pr>/comments \
  -F in_reply_to=<parent_databaseId> \
  -F body=$'Fixed in <commit-sha>: <one-line summary of the fix>'
```

**Resolve** the thread via GraphQL (uses the node id, not the database id):

```bash
gh api graphql -f query='
  mutation($threadId:ID!){
    resolveReviewThread(input:{threadId:$threadId}){ thread { isResolved } }
  }' -F threadId=<thread.id>
```

Only resolve threads where you actually landed a fix or replied with a justification the user has approved. Resolving without a real fix breaks the next reviewer's trust in the resolved-status signal.

### 7. Re-trigger Codex with `@codex review`

After every addressed thread has been replied to and resolved, post a fresh top-level comment on the PR with the literal body `@codex review`. This is required because Codex does **not** reliably re-trigger from a new push alone — sometimes the post-push hook fires, sometimes it doesn't, and waiting silently is indistinguishable from "Codex stalled". The explicit mention is the deterministic way to start the next pass.

```bash
gh pr comment <pr> --body "@codex review"
```

Only post this comment **after** the fix push and the thread replies/resolves have all landed — otherwise Codex starts the next pass against stale state and burns a full review cycle on outdated code.

If there's nothing to address but the loop is still waiting (e.g., 👀 was never added after a push), posting `@codex review` is also the right escalation before pinging the user.

### 8. Loop

Return to step 3 (snapshot) with refreshed `seen_*` sets. Codex's next pass picks up the comment within ~30–90 s, re-adds 👀, and either approves (👍) or surfaces new findings. Step 2 (initial `@codex review` trigger) is *not* repeated — step 7 already posted the trigger for the next pass.

## Polling cadence

Codex review runs typically complete in 1–5 minutes for a small PR, 10–20 for a large one. Pick the cadence by what you're waiting for:

- **First poll after push**: 60 seconds (Codex usually adds 👀 within 30–90 s).
- **Routine poll while 👀 is up**: 120 seconds (stays inside Anthropic's 5-min prompt cache window).
- **Routine poll while neither 👀 nor 👍 is present and no threads exist**: 120 seconds — Codex hasn't started yet (or finished a previous pass and a new one is pending).
- **👀 dropped + threads exist**: don't poll — switch immediately to the thread-handling subroutine.
- **No reaction at all after 5 minutes from push**: ping the user — Codex may not be configured for this repo, or the install is stalled. Also re-confirm the push actually landed.
- **👀 has been up >15 minutes with no thread activity**: ping the user — Codex may have stalled mid-run.

For dynamic-paced `/loop` runs, use `ScheduleWakeup` with `delaySeconds=120` (cache-warm) until 👍 lands. Don't poll faster than 60 seconds — Codex doesn't react that quickly anyway, and the prompt cache cost dominates.

**Prefer Bash `run_in_background` over Monitor for this loop.** The verdict we
wait for is a single terminal state (👍 approval, or 👀-dropped with new
findings) — exactly the "one notification when the condition is true" shape
Bash `run_in_background` is built for. Each iteration of the loop has exactly
one terminal condition to watch, so one completion notification per loop pass
is what we want.

Monitor is designed for ongoing event streams (one notification per
state-change line) — useful when you want to know about every transition in
real time, but notification delivery is best-effort and intermediate events
can drop. Real-world example from this skill's own bootstrap session: the 👀
→ 👍 transition fired correctly inside the monitor and was written to the
script's stdout file, but the follow-up `[change]` and `[approval]`
notifications never reached the conversation. The model sat waiting for an
event that had already fired.

Use the script below with `run_in_background: true` (not Monitor). It loops
until either terminal state, then exits with the verdict written to its last
stdout line so a single `Read` on the task's output file at notification time
recovers the verdict.

```bash
prev=""
while true; do
  reactions=$(gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<pr>/reactions \
    --jq '.[] | "\(.user.login):\(.content)"' 2>/dev/null | sort)
  threads=$(gh api graphql -f query='query($o:String!,$r:String!,$n:Int!){
    repository(owner:$o,name:$r){pullRequest(number:$n){
      reviewThreads(first:100){nodes{id isResolved comments(first:1){nodes{author{login}}}}}}}}' \
    -F o=<owner> -F r=<repo> -F n=<pr> \
    --jq '.data.repository.pullRequest.reviewThreads.nodes
          | map(select(.isResolved==false
                       and (.comments.nodes[0].author.login | test("codex"; "i"))))
          | length' 2>/dev/null)

  codex_eyes=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:eyes$" || true)
  codex_thumb=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:\+1$" || true)

  cur="reactions=[$reactions] threads=$threads"
  [ "$cur" != "$prev" ] && echo "[change] $cur"
  prev=$cur

  if [ -n "$codex_thumb" ]; then
    echo "VERDICT=approval"
    exit 0
  fi
  if [ -z "$codex_eyes" ] && [ "$threads" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "VERDICT=needs_action threads=$threads"
    exit 0
  fi

  sleep 120
done
```

When this script completes via Bash `run_in_background`, the single completion
notification fires. Recover the verdict with one `Read` on the task's output
file — the last line is `VERDICT=approval` or `VERDICT=needs_action threads=N`.
If the file's last line is something else (script crashed or hit the bash
timeout), apply the defensive re-check below.

### Defensive re-check: don't trust silence as "still reviewing"

Whether you use Bash `run_in_background` or `Monitor`, the delivery of
notifications back to the conversation is best-effort. If the runtime drops
the completion or change event, the loop sits waiting indefinitely for a
signal that already fired. The script's stdout file is authoritative;
the notification stream is convenience.

The mitigation is cheap and load-bearing: **after any unusually long quiet
period — more than ~10 minutes of total wait once you've seen 👀, or more
than ~5 minutes since the most recent event — do a direct verification
before continuing to wait or escalating to the user.** Verification is two
steps, in this order:

1. `Read` the background task's stdout file (the output path returned when
   the task started). If it contains a terminal line (the script's exit
   line, or an `[approval]` / `[needs_action]` event from a Monitor script)
   you missed the notification — act on it now.
2. Re-fetch PR state directly:
   ```bash
   gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<pr>/reactions \
     --jq '.[] | "\(.user.login):\(.content)"'
   ```
   If the output shows `:+1` (👍), or shows 👀 absent with unresolved Codex
   threads, the run completed and you missed the signal. Re-run the
   threads query and act on the verdict.

The general principle: **the script's stdout file is the source of truth
for detection; the notification stream is best-effort delivery.** Treat
the two as separate channels — the file is durable, the channel is lossy.

Codex is usually right but not always. When you read a thread and your read of the spec or code says Codex is wrong (or redundant, or stylistic-without-justification):

1. **Do not unilaterally dismiss it.** Stop and surface to the user with: what Codex says, what the spec / code says, your reasoning, and a recommendation (address as-is / push back / close-as-wontfix).
2. **The user picks.** Then follow through — if "push back", reply inline with the technical reasoning; if "close-as-wontfix", reply inline with the rationale before resolving; if "address", fall through to step 5 of the loop.
3. **Never reply inline arguing the point without checking with the user first.** Codex's threads are read by humans too — a wrong reply costs more than a one-minute clarification.

## Anti-patterns

- **Resolving threads without a real fix or explicit user disagreement.** Marking resolved is load-bearing — the next reviewer trusts it. An unaddressed finding hidden by a resolution is invisible until production.
- **Skipping the test** because "the fix is obvious." Obvious fixes regress just as often as subtle ones. The test exists to prove the fix held over time, not to prove the fix was needed today.
- **Replying with "fixed" only.** Quote the commit sha and a one-line summary so the thread is self-contained when read later.
- **Polling faster than 60 seconds.** Burns prompt cache for no benefit. Codex doesn't react that fast.
- **Continuing past a 👍 in case there are still threads.** 👍 means Codex is satisfied with the current state — pending threads are stale. Re-verify with the user before bulk-resolving.
- **Arguing with Codex inline without escalating.** A wrong reply is more expensive than a one-minute user clarification.
- **Hard-coding the bot login.** Different installs use different handles (`chatgpt-codex-connector`, `codex[bot]`, etc.). Match by case-insensitive substring instead.
- **Treating background-task notifications as a complete event log.** Whether you use Bash `run_in_background` or Monitor, the conversation-side notification stream is best-effort delivery — the script's stdout file is authoritative. After any quiet period of >5 minutes since the last event (or >10 minutes total since 👀 was seen), `Read` the task's output path *and* re-fetch reactions directly with `gh api .../reactions` before assuming "still reviewing." See "Defensive re-check" under Polling cadence.
- **Using Monitor when you only need a terminal verdict.** Monitor is designed for ongoing event streams (every state change becomes a notification). When the loop is waiting for a single terminal verdict — 👍 or new findings — Bash `run_in_background` is the right tool: one reliable completion notification, single `Read` of the output file to recover the verdict. Reach for Monitor only when you need real-time visibility into every intermediate state change.

## What this skill is not

- **A code-review skill.** Actual fixes come from project-specific implementation skills (`post-implementation-audit`, `test-driven-development`, `systematic-debugging`). This skill is the wrapper that decides *when* those skills fire and how the result is reported back to GitHub.
- **A merge skill.** 👍 doesn't auto-merge — the user owns the merge decision. Stop the loop on 👍, hand back, let them merge.
- **A way to delegate judgment back to Codex.** If you disagree with a finding, surface to the user. Don't argue with the bot.

When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

## SKILLS TO INVOKE

Always invoke with Skill() tool:
- Skill(rust-router) - First, to get routing
- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

content.content:
=== THREAD PRRT_kwDORp4enM6Gnbwo
file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/signature_pad/mod.rs:637  (reply_to=3345569439)
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Cancel drawing before rejecting disabled events**

When a parent flips `disabled` or `readonly` while the user is already drawing, `SyncProps` only updates the flags and leaves the machine in `State::Drawing` with `current_stroke` set; this guard then rejects the subsequent `DrawEnd` (and even `Clear`), so the pad stays stuck in the drawing state until it is re-enabled and can later commit a stale stroke. The sync path should cancel/finish the in-flight stroke or transition out of `Drawing` before disabled/read-only starts rejecting input.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

=== THREAD PRRT_kwDORp4enM6Gnbwq
file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/signature_pad/mod.rs:712  (reply_to=3345569442)
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Emit changes for controlled signature data**

When `Props::data` is set, `Bindable::set` only stages this new stroke list internally while `get()` and all exported/hidden-input attrs continue reading the unchanged controlled value until the parent syncs it back. Since `Props` has no change callback/effect for the adapter to notify the parent, drawing/undo/clear in controlled mode can leave the machine in `Completed` or `Idle` while `api.data()` and form submission still expose the old signature, making the advertised controlled `data` prop unusable.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

=== THREAD PRRT_kwDORp4enM6Gnbws
file: crates/ars-dom/src/signature_raster.rs:52  (reply_to=3345569444)
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Reject zero-sized raster exports before encoding**

In browser builds, a `RasterSpec` with `width == 0` or `height == 0` (for example exporting before the canvas has been measured or while it is hidden) makes `toDataURL` return the sentinel `data:,` rather than an encoded image. Because this code does not validate dimensions before calling the encoder, `decode_data_url` accepts that sentinel and returns `Ok(RasterImage { format: requested, bytes: [] })`, so callers can treat an empty byte buffer as a valid PNG/JPEG/WebP export.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

=== THREAD PRRT_kwDORp4enM6Gnbwt
file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/signature_pad/mod.rs:1026  (reply_to=3345569445)
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Disable the submitted input when the pad is disabled**

When a disabled signature pad is rendered inside a form with `name` set, this hidden input still carries the name/value and will be submitted even though the rest of the component is disabled. Other form-backed components in the repo mark their hidden inputs disabled for this case, so disabled signatures can unexpectedly submit stale data unless this attr mirrors `ctx.disabled`.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

=== THREAD PRRT_kwDORp4enM6Gnbwu
file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/signature_pad/mod.rs:65  (reply_to=3345569447)
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Treat empty strokes as blank signatures**

If a controlled/default value contains only empty strokes (which this module already tolerates by skipping them in `to_svg_path`), `is_empty()` returns false, so initialization enters `Completed`, the guide hides, and clear/undo become enabled even though the submitted/exported path is empty. This makes externally supplied or deserialized empty stroke data look like a real signature; emptiness should be based on recorded points or the input should be normalized before state selection.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

# Spec Directory Guide

## File Conventions

- `_category.md` files contain category overviews and shared conventions
- Component files use kebab-case matching the component name
- Shared types in `shared/` are referenced by component frontmatter
- Headings are numbered (`## 1. State Machine`, `### 1.1 States`)

## Spec Changes During Implementation

The repo is in active implementation. Spec edits inside `spec/` should usually be tied to a concrete GitHub implementation task or PR, not made as detached cleanup.

- Read the implementation issue before changing spec text.
- Keep cited spec sections synchronized with the implementation task that discovered the mismatch.
- If an adapter-specific implementation exposes a missing shared abstraction, promote that abstraction into the appropriate foundation/shared spec instead of documenting it only in framework code.
- Do not leave placeholder notes for later reconciliation; resolve the spec/code mismatch in the same task whenever possible.

## Adding a New Component

1. Create `components/{category}/{component-name}.md` with YAML frontmatter
2. Follow the section structure defined in `foundation/10-component-spec-template.md` for the appropriate tier
3. Add an entry to `manifest.toml` with path, category, deps, and related
4. Update `foundation/02-component-catalog.md` with the new component
5. Run `cargo run -p spec-tool -- validate` to verify consistency

## Component File Format

```markdown
---
component: ComponentName
category: { category }
tier: stateless | stateful | complex
foundation_deps: [architecture, accessibility, ...]
shared_deps: [date-time-types, ...]
related: [sibling-component, ...]
---

# ComponentName
```

See `foundation/10-component-spec-template.md` for the canonical section structure, ordering rules, conformance checklist, and skeleton examples for each tier.

content.text:
I've addressed all 5 Codex P2 findings with TDD and synced the specs. Summary of the fixes:

1. **Cancel drawing on disable** (mod.rs) — `SyncProps` now cancels the in-flight stroke and leaves `Drawing` when the pad becomes disabled/readonly mid-stroke, so it can't get stuck or commit a stale stroke.
2. **`on_data_change` callback** (mod.rs) — new `Props` callback + `Effect::DataChange` fired on commit/undo/clear, so controlled `data` is actually usable (parent observes changes, round-trips via `SyncData`).
3. **Reject zero-sized raster** (signature_raster.rs) — guards `width/height == 0` before encoding, returning `RasterError::Backend` instead of empty "valid" bytes from the `data:,` sentinel.
4. **Disable hidden input when disabled** (mod.rs) — disabled pad's hidden input gets `disabled` so it's excluded from form submission.
5. **Point-based `is_empty`** (raster/mod.rs) — emptiness counts recorded points, so data with only empty strokes is treated as blank (init→Idle, guide shown, clear/undo disabled).

New tests cover each (incl. a 5th wasm browser test for zero-size); spec synced across signature-pad.md, 11-dom-utilities §13. The second `cargo xci-fast` is running; once green I'll commit, push, reply inline + resolve all 5 threads, and re-trigger `@codex review`, looping until 👍.

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)

+-- Layer 1: [specific error]

- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake

- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-295
git add -A
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Address Codex review (PR #712)

Five P2 findings from the Codex review pass, each with tests:

- Cancel the in-flight stroke when `disabled`/`readonly` is set mid-stroke
  (`SyncProps`), so the machine leaves `Drawing` instead of being stranded
  where the disabled gate rejects the trailing `DrawEnd`/`Clear` and could
  later commit a stale stroke.
- Add `Props::on_data_change` + `Effect::DataChange`, fired on committed
  stroke / undo / clear, so controlled `data` is usable: the parent observes
  the change and feeds it back via props (`SyncData`). Not fired for
  parent-driven syncs; skipped when clearing an already-blank pad.
- Reject zero-sized raster specs in `WebSignatureRasterizer` before encoding —
  a 0×N canvas makes `toDataURL` return the `data:,` sentinel, which would
  otherwise decode to empty bytes mistakable for a valid image.
- Mark the hidden input `disabled` when the pad is disabled, so a disabled
  signature is excluded from form submission (matching other form-backed
  components).
- Make `SignatureData::is_empty` point-based (no recorded points) rather than
  stroke-count-based, so externally supplied data containing only empty
  strokes is treated as blank (init stays `Idle`, guide shown, clear/undo
  disabled), consistent with `to_svg_path`/`point_count`.

Specs synced: signature-pad.md (Props, Effect, transition, hidden input,
is_empty) and foundation/11-dom-utilities §13 (zero-size rejection).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git push 2>&1 | tail -4
echo "=== head commit ==="; git log --oneline -1

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-295
prev=""
while true; do
  reactions=$(gh api repos/fogodev/ars-ui/issues/712/reactions --jq '.[] | "\(.user.login):\(.content)"' 2>/dev/null | sort)
  threads=$(gh api graphql -f query='query($o:String!,$r:String!,$n:Int!){
    repository(owner:$o,name:$r){pullRequest(number:$n){
      reviewThreads(first:100){nodes{id isResolved comments(first:1){nodes{author{login}}}}}}}}' \
    -F o=fogodev -F r=ars-ui -F n=712 \
    --jq '.data.repository.pullRequest.reviewThreads.nodes
          | map(select(.isResolved==false and (.comments.nodes[0].author.login | test("codex"; "i"))))
          | length' 2>/dev/null)
  codex_eyes=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:eyes$" || true)
  codex_thumb=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:\+1$" || true)
  cur="reactions=[$reactions] threads=$threads"
  [ "$cur" != "$prev" ] && echo "[change] $cur"
  prev=$cur
  if [ -n "$codex_thumb" ]; then echo "VERDICT=approval"; exit 0; fi
  if [ -z "$codex_eyes" ] && [ "$threads" -gt 0 ]; then echo "VERDICT=needs_action threads=$threads"; exit 0; fi
  sleep 120
done
